story:
The ocean closes over Estrella’s head. In the blue below, big shapes turn toward the splash. “Ooo-ooo.” Knock. Knock. A massive gray whale glides beneath the waves with her newborn calf, and others cruise through the undersea blue. Bubbles ribbon past the girl. Something nudges. She lifts, breaks the surface, breathes. Slippery skin brushes her legs. She is not alone.

Two minutes earlier, Estrella—her name means Star—leaned over the wooden rail of the 1800s sailing ship Destiny’s Dreams. She and her parents, two sisters, and three brothers had sold their jungle-coast farm and were bound for San Francisco. She had cried when they left. “Everything I love is there—my friends. What future will I have in a strange place?” She didn’t know yet they would sail even farther north into a new wilderness.

“Ssshhh. Boom. Splash.” That morning she had pointed as a gray hump rose with a smaller one beside it. A mouth like a cave. Spray drifting. A spyhop to look around. A tail slap. Mama Gray Whale and her calf, born in warm southern seas, breathed and talked in low moans and hollow knocks. They nuzzled and cuddled, touching to bond.

A swell built miles away and rolled in, lifted the ship, and set it down askew. Estrella’s fingers slipped from the rail. Her feet skidded. Cool water. Panic. Cough. The hull rushed past. No one saw. Clothes dragged her down. Air. Air.

Then a bump, and her face rose into light. The mother whale, moved by a great surge of maternal instinct, lifted the strange small being as she lifted her own calf. Whales must breathe air; she supported both babies on her rostrum or along her great flipper. “Knock. Knock. Ooo-ooo.” Others answered, curious but gentle. They sifted tiny shrimp-like creatures from the kelp beds, never people. Estrella gulped a breath, sank, rose again. Her heart hammered. A round, fearless calf’s eye rolled to look at her. She touched the smooth gray skin and began to trust the steady rhythm that kept lifting her into air. The ship was turning. It was coming back.

High in the rigging, First Mate Adelberto froze. What's the strange voice? He scanned the glittering water. “Overboard! Overboard! Someone in the water.” Oars splashed. A strong island sailor slipped into the sea with a line and cork float. He became one with the water, reached Estrella, and towed her in. The mother whale rose close, blew—“Whoooosh”—her calf snug at her side. Estrella whispered “thank you,” and the whales slid down into the blue, their low calls and knocks braiding with the others’ underwater talk.

The whales traveled north with the ship. Sometimes the mother whale spyhopped to look. Sometimes she nudged the hull so near Estrella could almost touch her. It was a turning point. Estrella watched every day, eyes bright, hair streaming in the wind. Years later she became an expert on ocean life, keeping journals of the migrations and showing others how to watch from shore and from the decks of working boats. Now and then a gray whale came close, slapped its flukes, rolled an eye in its folded skin. “Is it you, Mama Whale?” Estrella would murmur. The sea answered with a gleam and a splash.

More than a century and a half afterward, in the attic of the Mystical Coast Lighthouse Keeper’s House, a ten-year-old boy named Lucas read from a faded journal they’d found in a sailor’s trunk: Voyages of the Destiny’s Dreams, by Estrella, Storyteller of the Blue Lantern. “A sea monster,” breathed the smallest listener. Outside, spring whales passed the kelp beds below the basalt cliffs. “Crash. Boom.” Sun poured in. Drawings of a whale, a lady, a ship. “Don’t tell anyone. These journals are our secrets,” Lucas whispered, and the words on the page seemed to lift like spray, carrying them back into the singing sea.
